title: Disorders of Lipoprotein Metabolism
authors: Alan Chait, Joseph L. Witztum, and Henry N. Ginsberg

items.title: Why LDL and Lp(a) elevation drive cardiovascular risk
items.text: Elevated LDL and Lp(a), together with mild-to-moderate hypertriglyceridemia, increase atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk; lowering LDL-C ameliorates or prevents events.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Elevations of low-density lipoproteins (LDL) and lipoprotein(a) (Lp[a]) and mild to moderate elevations of plasma triglycerides are associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, which can be ameliorated or prevented by lowering LDL-cholesterol (LDL-C) levels."

items.title: How intensified LDL lowering beyond statins reduces events
items.text: When statin monotherapy cannot reach guideline LDL targets, adding ezetimibe, PCSK9 inhibitors, or other agents that preserve hepatic LDL receptor activity further reduces cardiovascular events.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Aggressive lowering of LDL levels beyond that achievable with statins alone can further reduce the incidence of cardiovascular events."

items.title: Why severe hypertriglyceridemia mandates urgent TG reduction
items.text: Triglycerides above ~1000 mg/dL carry pancreatitis risk that outweighs chronic ASCVD concerns in the acute setting; marked TG lowering prevents recurrent pancreatitis.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Severe hypertriglyceridemia can result from several conditions and is associated with an increased risk of pancreatitis, which can be prevented by markedly reducing triglyceride levels."

items.title: Why raising HDL-C has not proven cardioprotective
items.text: Low HDL-C correlates with risk epidemiologically, but niacin and CETP inhibitor trials failed to reduce events despite HDL increases—HDL-C is not a validated therapeutic target.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Although low levels of high-density lipoprotein (HDL)-cholesterol (HDL-C) are associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, strategies to specifically increase HDL-C levels have not been effective to date in reducing cardiovascular disease events."

items.title: How lipoprotein classes differ in lipid composition
items.text: Chylomicrons and VLDL carry triglycerides; IDL, LDL, and Lp(a) are cholesterol-rich; HDL is phospholipid-enriched—classification underlies clinical interpretation of the lipid panel.
items.reference: Major Lipoproteins: "Chylomicrons, chylomicron remnants, and VLDLs are rich in TGs. Intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDLs), LDLs, and lipoprotein(a) (Lp[a]) are rich in cholesterol. HDLs are enriched in phospholipids."

items.title: How the LDL receptor clears plasma LDL
items.text: Hepatic LDL receptors bind apoB100 on LDL, cluster in coated pits, internalize particles, release ligand in acidic endosomes, and recycle to the surface—defective clearance raises plasma LDL-C.
items.reference: LDL Receptor: "When LDL receptors bind lipoproteins, they migrate to coated pits, and clathrin directs the complex to a cell membrane region that folds inward, creating an intracellular vesicle or endosome"

items.title: Why PCSK9 limits LDL receptor recycling
items.text: Secreted PCSK9 binds the hepatic LDL receptor after internalization and targets it for lysosomal degradation rather than surface recycling—gain-of-function PCSK9 mutations cause familial hypercholesterolemia.
items.reference: LDL Receptor: "In the presence of PCSK9 (see Fig. 41.13 and “Proprotein Convertase Subtilisin/Kexin Type 9” section), the LDL receptor conformation is altered, promoting degradation and preventing recycling to the cell surface."

items.title: How FH arises from LDLR, APOB, and PCSK9 defects
items.text: Most FH is autosomal dominant LDLR loss-of-function; familial defective apoB reduces receptor binding; rare PCSK9 gain-of-function accelerates receptor degradation—all impair hepatic LDL clearance.
items.reference: Monogenic Familial Hypercholesterolemia: "Among patients with FH, 80% to 85% of cases are caused by loss-of-function mutations in the LDLR gene, and another 5% to 10% are caused by missense mutations in the LDL receptor-binding domain of APOB-100 that reduce the ability of LDL to bind to hepatic LDL receptors (familial defective APOB [FDB])."

items.title: How statins lower LDL-C
items.text: Statins inhibit hepatic HMG-CoA reductase, deplete intracellular cholesterol, upregulate SREBP-driven LDL receptor expression, and enhance LDL clearance—the cornerstone of ASCVD prevention.
items.reference: Cholesterol Metabolism: "Statins, which are the major LDL-lowering agents, work by inhibiting cholesterol biosynthesis in the hepatocyte, thereby lowering intracellular cholesterol levels, which results in an increase in LDL receptor expression and transport to the hepatocyte cell surface, where they bind and internalize plasma LDL, thereby enhancing LDL removal"

items.title: How ezetimibe blocks intestinal cholesterol absorption
items.text: Ezetimibe binds NPC1L1 on enterocytes, reducing dietary and biliary cholesterol reabsorption; added to statins it lowers LDL-C ~14–25% and reduces cardiovascular events.
items.reference: Cholesterol Absorption, Synthesis, and Excretion: "NPC1L1 is the target of ezetimibe, a drug that lowers cholesterol and has been shown to decrease CVD."

items.title: How monoclonal PCSK9 inhibitors augment LDL clearance
items.text: Alirocumab and evolocumab prevent PCSK9-mediated LDL receptor degradation, allowing receptor recycling and ~60% additional LDL lowering on statins, with proven ASCVD event reduction.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "Clinical trials with both monoclonal antibody PCSK9 inhibitors (alirocumab and evolocumab) marketed in the United States demonstrate reductions in atherosclerotic CVD events, particularly in patients with recent acute coronary syndrome, multivessel CAD, or peripheral arterial disease."

items.title: How inclisiran silences hepatic PCSK9 synthesis
items.text: GalNAc-conjugated siRNA inclisiran is given subcutaneously at baseline, 3 months, then every 6 months, lowering LDL-C ~51% by reducing hepatic PCSK9 production.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "A meta-analysis of data from three randomized clinical trials demonstrated that inclisiran decreases LDL-C levels by 51% and is associated with a 24% lower risk of major adverse cardiovascular events."

items.title: How bempedoic acid lowers LDL in statin-intolerant patients
items.text: Bempedoic acid inhibits ATP-citrate lyase upstream of HMG-CoA reductase, reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and upregulating LDL receptors—useful alone or combined when statins are not tolerated.
items.reference: Bempedoic Acid: "Thus, bempedoic acid inhibits cholesterol synthesis, which in turn leads to an increase in hepatic LDL receptor synthesis and reduced plasma LDL-C."

items.subtopic: Lipoprotein(a) as a Major Cardiovascular Risk Factor
items.title: Why Lp(a) is an independent genetic ASCVD risk factor
items.text: Lp(a) levels are ~90% genetically determined; elevated Lp(a) confers MI, stroke, PAD, and aortic stenosis risk independent of LDL-C, yet no approved Lp(a)-specific therapy exists.
items.reference: Lipoprotein(a) as a Major Cardiovascular Risk Factor: "In fact, Lp(a) is probably the most prevalent lipoprotein risk factor worldwide for CVD for which no specific therapy is currently available."

items.title: Why APOC2 is required for triglyceride clearance
items.text: LPL hydrolyzes TG on chylomicrons and VLDL at capillary endothelium; APOC2 is an obligate LPL cofactor—biallelic APOC2 loss causes severe hypertriglyceridemia mimicking LPL deficiency.
items.reference: Lipoprotein Lipase: "Apolipoprotein C2 (APOC2), encoded by APOC2, is an obligate activator of LPL."

items.title: How severe hypertriglyceridemia causes pancreatitis
items.text: TG >1000 mg/dL can precipitate chylomicronemia syndrome; pancreatic lipolysis of retained chylomicrons may generate toxic lysolecithin and free fatty acids, triggering acute pancreatitis with worse outcomes than other causes.
items.reference: Chylomicronemia Syndrome: "However, by far the most serious consequence of severe hypertriglyceridemia is acute pancreatitis, which can be recurrent if TG levels are not adequately managed."

items.subtopic: Drug Treatment for Mild to Moderately Severe Hypertriglyceridemia
items.title: How fibrates lower triglycerides and raise HDL
items.text: Fibrates activate PPARα, increasing LPL activity and apoA-I/A-II while lowering APOC3—typically reducing TG 25–50% and raising HDL-C 5–20%; fenofibrate is preferred with statins over gemfibrozil.
items.reference: Fibrates: "Importantly, fibrates lower TGs by 25% to 50% and raise HDL-C by 5% to 20%, depending on the baseline levels of TGs."

items.title: How icosapent ethyl reduced ASCVD in REDUCE-IT
items.text: Prescription EPA (icosapent ethyl 4 g/day) reduced major coronary events in statin-treated patients with mild hypertriglyceridemia and established ASCVD or diabetes—distinct from mixed EPA/DHA trials that were neutral.
items.reference: Omega-3 Fatty Acids: "However, a more recent study, Reduction of Cardiovascular Events With Icosapent Ethyl–Intervention Trial (REDUCE-IT), using 4 g of icosapent ethyl, a synthetic EPA, in mildly to moderately hypertriglyceridemic participants with known atherosclerosis or high risk (e.g., diabetes), dramatically reduced coronary events."

items.title: Why niacin use has declined despite lipid effects
items.text: Niacin lowers TG and raises HDL but AIM-HIGH and HPS2-THRIVE showed no added benefit atop statins; flushing and hepatotoxicity limit use—now reserved for refractory hypertriglyceridemia or extreme Lp(a).
items.reference: Niacin: "However, more recent trials of slow-release niacin in subjects whose LDL was already markedly reduced by statins failed to show additional clinical benefit with niacin,"

items.title: How familial combined hyperlipidemia presents
items.text: FCHL features concurrent LDL and VLDL elevation from hepatic APOB overproduction (often with insulin resistance); phenotypes within families vary between isolated high TG, high LDL, or both over time.
items.reference: Combined Hyperlipoproteinemias: "When it occurs within families, this phenotypic pattern has historically been termed familial combined hyperlipidemia (FCHL)."

items.title: Why Tangier disease causes near-absent HDL
items.text: Biallelic ABCA1 loss impairs cholesterol efflux to apoA-I, producing extremely low HDL-C, orange tonsils, and hepatosplenomegaly; paradoxically LDL-C is also very low and major ASCVD risk is not clearly increased.
items.reference: Tangier Disease: "Tangier disease results from biallelic homozygous loss-of-function or compound heterozygous mutations in the ABCA1 gene."

items.title: How CETP reshapes HDL and LDL composition
items.text: CETP exchanges cholesteryl esters from HDL for TG on VLDL/IDL/LDL, enriching atherogenic particles with cholesterol in humans—CETP deficiency raises HDL-C and lowers LDL-C.
items.reference: Cholesteryl Ester Transfer Protein: "CETP promotes the exchange between lipoproteins of two classes of neutral lipids: cholesteryl esters and TGs."

items.title: How diabetes causes atherogenic dyslipidemia
items.text: Insulin deficiency/resistance increases VLDL secretion, impairs LPL, and drives CETP-mediated formation of small dense LDL and low HDL—APOB is elevated even when LDL-C appears normal.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus: "Diabetic dyslipidemia is characterized by moderate hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL-C, and an increased number of small LDL particles such that APOB, but not LDL-C, is elevated."

items.title: Why untreated hypothyroidism elevates LDL and TG
items.text: Hypothyroidism reduces hepatic LDL receptors, bile acid synthesis, LPL activity, and CETP—restoring euthyroidism normalizes lipids and should be excluded before intensifying lipid drugs.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "Uncontrolled hypothyroidism usually causes hypercholesterolemia, often accompanied by hypertriglyceridemia."

items.title: How ASCVD risk guides LDL-C treatment targets
items.text: Secondary prevention CAD patients should reach LDL-C <70 mg/dL (European guidelines often <55 mg/dL in very high risk); primary prevention uses risk calculators and treats LDL-C >190 mg/dL regardless of score.
items.reference: Patient Selection and Treatment Goals: "Almost all guidelines now agree that the LDL-C goal for secondary prevention in patients with existing CAD should be less than 70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L)."

items.subtopic: Laboratory Analysis of Lipids and Lipoproteins
items.title: Why calculated LDL-C fails at high triglycerides
items.text: Friedewald LDL-C becomes inaccurate when TG exceeds ~400 mg/dL and misclassifies risk when discordant from non–HDL-C; direct LDL or beta-quantification is needed in severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.reference: Laboratory Analysis of Lipids and Lipoproteins: "LDL-C measured by the Friedewald equation can be inaccurate in the presence of hypertriglyceridemia, and the standard lipid panel is not recommended for use with TGs over 400 mg/dL (4.5 mmol/L), although inaccuracies are present even at lower TG levels."

items.question: A 12-year-old with LDL-C 420 mg/dL, Achilles tendon thickening, and a parent with premature MI most likely has:
items.explanation: FH is the commonest monogenic hypercholesterolemia with tendon xanthomas and very high LDL from impaired receptor-mediated clearance.
items.reference: Monogenic Familial Hypercholesterolemia: "Patients with FH have up to a 10-fold increased prevalence of CAD if untreated."

items.question: A patient on maximally tolerated statin plus ezetimibe has LDL-C 95 mg/dL with recent ACS. Best additional therapy?
items.explanation: PCSK9 inhibitors are recommended when LDL remains ≥70 mg/dL on maximally tolerated oral therapy in high-risk ASCVD patients.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "PCSK9 inhibitors are recommended for high-risk patients with LDL levels of 70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L) or higher on maximally tolerated oral therapies, including statins and/or ezetimibe."

items.question: Ezetimibe lowers LDL-C primarily by:
items.explanation: Ezetimibe targets the intestinal sterol transporter NPC1L1, complementing statin-mediated synthesis blockade.
items.reference: Cholesterol Absorption, Synthesis, and Excretion: "NPC1L1 is the target of ezetimibe, a drug that lowers cholesterol and has been shown to decrease CVD."

items.question: Bempedoic acid is especially useful in which clinical scenario?
options: Statin-intolerant patient needing additional LDL lowering
Familial chylomicronemia with absent LPL activity
items.explanation: Bempedoic acid inhibits ATP-citrate lyase, reduces LDL-C 18–30%, and is active in the liver without systemic statin exposure.
items.reference: Bempedoic Acid: "It may be especially useful in statin-intolerant patients."

items.subtopic: Lipoprotein(a) as a Major Cardiovascular Risk Factor
items.question: A patient with LDL-C at goal on statin has Lp(a) 180 nmol/L and family history of early MI. Best approach?
options: Treat Lp(a) as a risk-enhancing factor and intensify LDL lowering; measure Lp(a) once in relatives
Stop statin because statins always lower Lp(a)
Ignore Lp(a) if LDL-C is at target
Start fibrate solely to lower Lp(a)
items.explanation: Elevated Lp(a) amplifies baseline ASCVD risk; maximize LDL-C lowering and address other risk factors—no approved Lp(a)-specific drug yet.
items.reference: Management of the Patient With High Lp(a): "Thus, all CVD risk factors should be optimally addressed according to the various guideline recommendations."

items.question: A patient presents with TG 2800 mg/dL and epigastric pain. Immediate priority beyond IV fluids?
options: Aggressive triglyceride lowering (diet, fibrate, omega-3) to prevent recurrent pancreatitis
items.explanation: Severe hypertriglyceridemia causes acute pancreatitis; target TG <500 mg/dL to minimize recurrence risk.
items.reference: Drug Treatment for Severe Hypertriglyceridemia: "The initial goal of drug treatment for the patient with severe hypertriglyceridemia is to prevent the development of TG-induced pancreatitis, the onset of which will not occur at levels below 500 to 750 mg/dL (5.6–8.5 mmol/L)."

items.question: A child with fasting TG >5000 mg/dL and absent post-heparin LPL activity has:
items.explanation: FCS features absent LPL activity, extreme fasting hyperchylomicronemia, and pancreatitis risk from childhood.
items.reference: Genetics of Severe Hypertriglyceridemia: "FCS, also known as type I hyperlipoproteinemia or LPL deficiency, is an extremely rare autosomal-recessive genetic disorder characterized by absent plasma activity of LPL, which results in marked degrees of hypertriglyceridemia."

items.question: Biallelic APOC2 deficiency causes severe hypertriglyceridemia because:
options: APOC2 is an obligate cofactor required to activate LPL
APOC2 is the LDL receptor ligand on LDL particles
items.explanation: Without APOC2, LPL cannot hydrolyze TG on chylomicrons and VLDL, mimicking LPL deficiency.
items.reference: Apolipoproteins C1, C2, and C3: "APOC2 plays a vital role in mediating TG clearance from plasma because it is an obligate activator of the enzyme LPL."

items.question: When combining a fibrate with a statin for hypertriglyceridemia, which agent is preferred to minimize myopathy risk?
items.explanation: Gemfibrozil inhibits statin glucuronidation and raises myopathy risk; fenofibrate does not significantly affect statin metabolism.
items.reference: Fibrates: "The combination of gemfibrozil and most statins is associated with an increased risk of myopathy due to increased statin blood levels, whereas fenofibrate does not interfere with statin metabolism and is preferred in fibrate/statin combination regimens."

items.question: REDUCE-IT demonstrated cardiovascular benefit with:
options: Icosapent ethyl 4 g/day added to statin therapy
Combined EPA+DHA that was stopped for futility
Dietary fish intake alone without statin
items.explanation: Prescription EPA (icosapent ethyl) reduced coronary events in high-risk patients with TG 135–500 mg/dL on statins.
items.reference: Omega-3 Fatty Acids: "Nonetheless, icosapent ethyl has been approved to reduce CVD in people with TG levels greater than 135 mg/dL (1.5 mmol/L), although it was not related to a decrease in plasma TG levels."

items.question: Familial combined hyperlipidemia is best characterized by:
options: Elevated LDL and VLDL with variable TG/LDL pattern in family members
Isolated Lp(a) elevation with normal LDL
Homozygous APOE2 causing palmar xanthomas only
items.explanation: FCHL reflects hepatic APOB overproduction with combined LDL and TG elevation and variable expression within pedigrees.
items.reference: Combined Hyperlipoproteinemias: "Combined hyperlipoproteinemia usually refers to the simultaneous elevation of LDL and VLDL levels."

items.question: Orange hypertrophic tonsils and HDL-C <5 mg/dL suggest:
options: LCAT deficiency with corneal opacity only
CETP deficiency with very high HDL
items.explanation: Tangier disease features absent ABCA1-mediated cholesterol efflux, near-absent HDL, and characteristic tonsillar lipid deposition.
items.reference: Tangier Disease: "Patients with Tangier disease have enlarged yellow- or orange-colored tonsils and enlarged livers, spleens, and lymph nodes, presumably due to inability to export cholesterol from tissue macrophages."

items.question: Homozygous CETP deficiency typically produces:
options: Markedly elevated HDL-C and reduced LDL-C
Low HDL and high Lp(a) only
items.explanation: Loss of CETP impairs cholesteryl ester transfer from HDL to apoB lipoproteins, raising HDL-C and lowering LDL-C.
items.reference: CETP Deficiency: "Homozygous mutations in the CETP gene that result in complete loss of enzyme activity lead to increased HDL-C and reduced LDL-C levels because of impaired transfer of cholesteryl esters from HDLs to lower-density lipoproteins."

items.question: A patient with type 2 diabetes has TG 280 mg/dL, HDL 34 mg/dL, and LDL-C 98 mg/dL. The most atherogenic feature is often:
options: Elevated APOB from increased small dense LDL particles
items.explanation: Diabetic dyslipidemia elevates atherogenic particle number (APOB) despite seemingly normal LDL-C.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus: "Diabetic dyslipidemia is characterized by moderate hypertriglyceridemia, low HDL-C, and an increased number of small LDL particles such that APOB, but not LDL-C, is elevated."

items.question: A patient with new hypercholesterolemia and fatigue has TSH 28 mIU/L. Before starting ezetimibe, you should:
items.explanation: Secondary hypercholesterolemia from hypothyroidism often normalizes with levothyroxine—exclude treatable causes first.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "Restoration of the euthyroid status leads to a return of lipid levels to their baseline status."

items.subtopic: Chronic Kidney Disease and Nephrotic Syndrome
items.question: Nephrotic syndrome commonly causes dyslipidemia through:
options: Increased APOB synthesis (raising LDL) and elevated ANGPTL4 (raising TG)
items.explanation: Nephrotic syndrome raises LDL via increased hepatic APOB production and TG via ANGPTL4-mediated LPL inhibition.
items.reference: Chronic Kidney Disease and Nephrotic Syndrome: "Hypertriglyceridemia in nephrotic syndrome has been linked to increased circulating levels of the LPL inhibitor ANGPTL4."

items.question: For secondary prevention in established coronary artery disease, current guidelines agree on an LDL-C target of:
options: <70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L), with lower goals in very high-risk patients
items.explanation: Secondary prevention CAD warrants LDL-C <70 mg/dL; European guidelines often target <55 mg/dL in very high risk.
items.reference: Patient Selection and Treatment Goals: "Almost all guidelines now agree that the LDL-C goal for secondary prevention in patients with existing CAD should be less than 70 mg/dL (1.8 mmol/L)."

items.question: A patient reports muscle aches on atorvastatin 80 mg. Creatine kinase is normal. Next step per chapter guidance:
options: Exclude hypothyroidism and interacting drugs; consider lower dose or alternate-day rosuvastatin
Switch immediately to high-dose niacin monotherapy
items.explanation: Most statin myalgia occurs with normal CK; evaluate secondary causes and drug interactions before abandoning therapy.
items.reference: HMG-CoA Reductase Inhibitors (Statins): "In patients with muscle complaints after statin administration, creatinine kinase levels will be within normal limits in over 99% of cases."

items.question: Gain-of-function PCSK9 mutations cause hypercholesterolemia by:
options: Enhancing lysosomal degradation of hepatic LDL receptors
Blocking APOB100 synthesis in the liver
items.explanation: PCSK9 binds internalized LDL receptors and diverts them to degradation rather than recycling, reducing hepatic LDL clearance.
items.reference: Gain-of-Function Mutations in PCSK9: "Gain-of-function mutations increase the ability of the secreted PCSK9 to mediate enhanced degradation of hepatic LDL receptors, reducing LDL receptor number, which leads to reduced plasma LDL clearance by the liver and a clinical picture of FH."

items.question: A middle-aged man with TG 1600 mg/dL on prednisone and uncontrolled diabetes most likely has:
options: Multifactorial chylomicronemia syndrome exacerbated by secondary factors
items.explanation: MFCS results from polygenic hypertriglyceridemia worsened by drugs/metabolic conditions that saturate TG removal pathways.
items.reference: Causes and Pathogenesis: "MFCS is nearly always the result of exacerbation of genetic forms of mild to moderate hypertriglyceridemia by disorders or drugs that can adversely affect TG levels"

items.question: Inclisiran differs from alirocumab/evolocumab because it:
options: Uses hepatic siRNA to reduce PCSK9 synthesis with dosing every 6 months
Is an oral MTP inhibitor taken daily
items.explanation: Inclisiran is a GalNAc-conjugated siRNA given subcutaneously at extended intervals to silence hepatic PCSK9.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "injections of inclisiran are given at baseline, 3 months, and then only at 6-month intervals."

items.subtopic: Laboratory Analysis of Lipids and Lipoproteins
items.question: When fasting TG is 520 mg/dL, calculated LDL-C by Friedewald is often:
options: Underestimated or unreliable; direct LDL or non–HDL-C preferred
More accurate than at low TG
items.explanation: Friedewald LDL-C is inaccurate when TG >400 mg/dL; non–HDL-C or direct assays are preferred.
items.reference: Laboratory Analysis of Lipids and Lipoproteins: "the standard lipid panel is not recommended for use with TGs over 400 mg/dL (4.5 mmol/L), although inaccuracies are present even at lower TG levels."

items.question: Extended-release niacin added to statin in patients with LDL already at goal failed to show benefit primarily because:
options: AIM-HIGH and HPS2-THRIVE showed no incremental CVD reduction despite HDL/TG changes
Niacin is contraindicated with any statin
items.explanation: Recent outcome trials found no added event reduction from niacin atop statins despite favorable lipid shifts.
items.reference: Niacin: "However, more recent trials of slow-release niacin in subjects whose LDL was already markedly reduced by statins failed to show additional clinical benefit with niacin,"

items.question: The central causal lipoprotein in atherosclerosis development is:
options: LDL, particularly when elevated over a lifetime
Chylomicrons in all adults regardless of TG
Lp(a) only when LDL is very low
items.explanation: LDL-C is the dominant causal factor for atherosclerotic CVD; lowering LDL prevents and regresses disease.
items.reference: Hypercholesterolemia and Atherosclerosis: "Similarly, there is now overwhelming epidemiologic, experimental, and therapeutic data that plasma cholesterol, and specifically LDL-C, is the major causal factor responsible for the complications of atherosclerosis"

items.question: A woman with baseline TG 380 mg/dL starting oral estrogen for menopause is at risk of:
options: Severe hypertriglyceridemia and pancreatitis if she has underlying genetic susceptibility
Isolated LDL lowering without TG change
items.explanation: Oral estrogen increases hepatic VLDL production and can precipitate chylomicronemia in predisposed patients—check TG before therapy.
items.reference: Drugs Causing Hyper- and Dyslipoproteinemia: "In the setting of an underlying genetic form of hypertriglyceridemia, severe hyperchylomicronemia and pancreatitis can occur in patients taking oral estrogen alone or estrogen-containing birth control pills"

items.question: Dietary management of familial chylomicronemia syndrome requires:
options: Total fat restriction to <5–10 g/day (often with MCT supplementation)
High saturated fat to stimulate LPL
Unrestricted omega-3 fish oil at 4 g/day as sole therapy
High carbohydrate diet to lower VLDL
items.explanation: FCS lacks functional LPL; all dietary fat must be severely restricted, with MCTs used because they bypass chylomicron formation.
items.reference: Approach to Management of the Patient With Severe Hypertriglyceridemia—Diet and Lifestyle Measures: "in FCS, total dietary fat (all types, including saturated and unsaturated) intake should be limited to less than 5 to 10 g/day, which is difficult to maintain on an ongoing basis and is a major burden to patients."

items.statement: Lowering LDL-cholesterol can ameliorate or prevent cardiovascular disease associated with dyslipidemia.
items.explanation: Key points state LDL-C lowering prevents or ameliorates CVD linked to LDL, Lp(a), and moderate TG elevations.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "which can be ameliorated or prevented by lowering LDL-cholesterol (LDL-C) levels."

items.statement: Strategies to specifically increase HDL-C have been effective in reducing cardiovascular disease events to date.
items.explanation: Key points explicitly state HDL-raising strategies have not reduced CVD events.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "strategies to specifically increase HDL-C levels have not been effective to date in reducing cardiovascular disease events."

items.statement: Apolipoprotein C2 is an obligate activator of lipoprotein lipase.
items.explanation: APOC2 is required as cofactor for LPL-mediated TG hydrolysis.
items.reference: Lipoprotein Lipase: "Apolipoprotein C2 (APOC2), encoded by APOC2, is an obligate activator of LPL."

items.statement: Heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia affects approximately 1 in 250 people.
items.explanation: Updated population data suggest HeFH prevalence ~1:250, higher than historic 1:500 estimates.
items.reference: Monogenic Familial Hypercholesterolemia: "Although the prevalence of HeFH was previously thought to be about 1:500, more recent population data suggest a prevalence of about 1:250."

items.statement: Statins lower lipoprotein(a) levels by approximately 10% on average.
items.explanation: Statins do not lower Lp(a); they may raise Lp(a) ~10% while lowering LDL-C.
items.reference: Management of the Patient With High Lp(a): "Statins do not lower Lp(a) and, in fact, can raise Lp(a) by approximately 10%, but this increase can be up to 20%."

items.statement: Acute pancreatitis is the most serious consequence of severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: Chylomicronemia syndrome's gravest complication is TG-induced pancreatitis.
items.reference: Chylomicronemia Syndrome: "by far the most serious consequence of severe hypertriglyceridemia is acute pancreatitis"

items.statement: Fenofibrate is preferred over gemfibrozil when combined with statins because fenofibrate does not interfere with statin metabolism.
items.explanation: Gemfibrozil raises statin levels and myopathy risk; fenofibrate lacks this interaction.
items.reference: Fibrates: "whereas fenofibrate does not interfere with statin metabolism and is preferred in fibrate/statin combination regimens."

items.statement: Familial combined hyperlipidemia can result from increased VLDL secretion leading to elevated LDL production.
items.explanation: Hepatic overproduction of APOB-containing lipoproteins underlies combined LDL/VLDL elevation in FCHL.
items.reference: Etiology of Hypercholesterolemia: "Increased LDL-C also can result from increased VLDL secretion leading to increased LDL production, as occurs in familial combined hyperlipidemia"

items.statement: Tangier disease is caused by mutations in the ABCA1 gene.
items.explanation: ABCA1 deficiency impairs cholesterol efflux to apoA-I, causing Tangier disease.
items.reference: Tangier Disease: "Tangier disease results from biallelic homozygous loss-of-function or compound heterozygous mutations in the ABCA1 gene."

items.statement: Diabetes mellitus is a prominent cause of hypertriglyceridemia.
items.explanation: Insulin resistance increases VLDL secretion and impairs TG clearance in diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus: "Diabetes mellitus is a prominent cause of hypertriglyceridemia."

items.statement: Most guidelines recommend drug treatment for any patient with LDL-C greater than 190 mg/dL irrespective of risk score.
items.explanation: LDL-C ≥190 mg/dL warrants therapy even without calculated risk score elevation.
items.reference: Patient Selection and Treatment Goals: "Most guidelines recommend drug treatment for any patient with LDL-C greater than 190 mg/dL (4.9 mmol/L), irrespective of risk score."

items.statement: Fibrates are largely effective in lowering triglycerides in familial chylomicronemia syndrome.
items.explanation: FCS lacks LPL activity; fibrates that work via LPL upregulation are largely ineffective.
items.reference: Familial Chylomicronemia Syndrome: "Fibrates, which effectively lower TGs in most situations, are largely ineffective in lowering TGs in FCS."

items.subtopic: Lipoprotein(a) as a Major Cardiovascular Risk Factor
items.statement: Lp(a) levels are approximately 90% determined by genetic variation at the LPA locus.
items.explanation: Lp(a) is highly heritable with minimal adult environmental influence.
items.reference: Regulation of Lp(a) Levels: "levels are approximately 90% determined by genetic variation at the LPA locus."

items.assertion: Assertion: PCSK9 promotes degradation of the hepatic LDL receptor.
items.reason: Reason: PCSK9 is synthesized only in adipose tissue and has no hepatic role.
items.explanation: Assertion true—PCSK9 targets LDL receptor for lysosomal degradation; reason false—hepatic PCSK9 secretion regulates plasma LDL.
items.reference: Proprotein Convertase Subtilisin/Kexin Type 9: "Although it is secreted by a number of tissues, it is the hepatic secretion that is of relevance to regulation of plasma LDL concentrations."

items.assertion: Assertion: Patients with homozygous FH may have LDL-C exceeding 1000 mg/dL.
items.reason: Reason: Homozygous FH results from two normal LDLR alleles with enhanced receptor activity.
items.explanation: Assertion true—HoFH causes extreme LDL-C; reason false—HoFH requires two defective LDLR (or equivalent) alleles.
items.reference: Monogenic Familial Hypercholesterolemia: "and even exceeding 1000 mg/dL (25.9 mmol/L) in those with HoFH."

items.assertion: Assertion: Statins are the cornerstone of LDL-lowering therapy.
items.reason: Reason: Statins work by increasing intestinal cholesterol absorption via NPC1L1.
items.explanation: Assertion true—statins are first-line for LDL lowering; reason false—that describes ezetimibe, not statins.
items.reference: HMG-CoA Reductase Inhibitors (Statins): "Statins are the cornerstone of therapy to lower LDL-C and have been in use since 1984."

items.assertion: Assertion: PCSK9 monoclonal antibodies can lower LDL-C by nearly 60% beyond statins alone.
items.reason: Reason: PCSK9 inhibitors block hepatic VLDL secretion entirely.
items.explanation: Assertion true—PCSK9 antibodies markedly augment LDL lowering; reason false—they preserve LDL receptors, not block VLDL secretion.
items.reference: PCSK9 Inhibitors: "leading to a nearly 60% further reduction in circulating LDL beyond that achieved by statins alone"

items.subtopic: Lipoprotein(a) as a Major Cardiovascular Risk Factor
items.assertion: Assertion: Elevated Lp(a) is an independent causal risk factor for cardiovascular disease.
items.reason: Reason: Lp(a) levels are primarily determined by dietary saturated fat intake.
items.explanation: Assertion true—genetic and epidemiologic data support Lp(a) causality; reason false—~90% of Lp(a) is genetically determined.
items.reference: Lp(a) Epidemiology: "have established that Lp(a) is an independent, causal, and genetic risk factor for CVD in both primary and secondary settings."

items.assertion: Assertion: Biallelic APOC2 deficiency causes severe hypertriglyceridemia.
items.reason: Reason: APOC2 is an inhibitor of lipoprotein lipase activity.
items.explanation: Assertion true—APOC2 loss mimics LPL deficiency; reason false—APOC2 activates LPL; APOC3 inhibits LPL.
items.reference: Apolipoproteins C1, C2, and C3: "Humans with biallelic loss-of-function mutations of APOC2, which is extremely rare, have severe hypertriglyceridemia, mimicking LPL deficiency."

items.assertion: Assertion: Severe hypertriglyceridemia can cause acute pancreatitis.
items.reason: Reason: Pancreatitis only occurs when triglycerides are below 200 mg/dL.
items.explanation: Assertion true—TG-induced pancreatitis rises with levels >1000 mg/dL; reason false—risk increases at high, not low, TG.
items.reference: Chylomicronemia Syndrome: "However, by far the most serious consequence of severe hypertriglyceridemia is acute pancreatitis"

items.assertion: Assertion: Icosapent ethyl reduced coronary events in the REDUCE-IT trial.
items.reason: Reason: REDUCE-IT used a combined EPA and DHA preparation stopped for futility.
items.explanation: Assertion true—icosapent ethyl (EPA alone) showed benefit; reason false—the futility trial used EPA+DHA, not icosapent ethyl.
items.reference: Omega-3 Fatty Acids: "Importantly, a study using a similar design but with an agent that contained both EPA and DHA was stopped early for futility."

items.assertion: Assertion: Tangier disease causes near absence of HDL-C.
items.reason: Reason: Tangier disease results from CETP overactivity.
items.explanation: Assertion true—ABCA1 loss abolishes HDL; reason false—Tangier is ABCA1 deficiency, not CETP excess.
items.reference: Tangier Disease: "Although affected individuals have a near absence of HDL-C due to the inability to transport cellular FC to APOA1"

items.reason: Reason: Hypothyroidism increases hepatic LDL receptor number.
items.explanation: Assertion true—hypothyroidism raises LDL and TG; reason false—it reduces LDL receptor expression.
items.reference: Hypothyroidism: "Mechanisms include a reduction in LDL receptors, reduced cholesterol  $ 7\alpha $-hydroxylase, low CETP activity, and decreased LPL."

items.assertion: Assertion: The adage 'the lower the LDL, the better' guides treatment in high-risk patients.
items.reason: Reason: Recent trials show no benefit from LDL-C below 70 mg/dL.
items.explanation: Assertion true—aggressive LDL lowering regresses plaque; reason false—trials support benefit below 70 mg/dL including <50 mg/dL.
items.reference: Hypercholesterolemia and Atherosclerosis: "Thus, the adage that “the lower the LDL, the better” is now the guideline for treatment goals in high-risk patients."

items.subtopic: Secondary Forms of Hyper- and Dyslipoproteinemia
items.assertion: Assertion: Treating underlying diabetes can improve diabetic dyslipidemia.
items.reason: Reason: Diabetes causes dyslipidemia solely through CETP deficiency.
items.explanation: Assertion true—glycemic control improves TG and particle profile; reason false—diabetes dyslipidemia involves VLDL overproduction and impaired LPL, not CETP loss.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus: "Although patients with diabetes do not have increased LDL-C compared with age- and sex-matched controls, improved diabetes control can be associated with reduced LDL-C levels and, importantly, reduced numbers of atherogenic particles."
